personality_belief.metaphysical_profile: Used only as cultural flavor, never as a decision rule.
personality_belief.decision_style: practical and constrained by real workflow trade-offs
personality_belief.trust_orientation: needs proof from concrete workflows, not just positioning
personality_belief.self_image: responsible, busy, and careful about adding complexity

economic_profile.purchase_decision_process: tests fit against current workflow before committing

life_goals: stabilize workflow to reduce evening catch-up
fears: investing time in a tool that requires complex migration
appearing incompetent to management due to disorganization
financial loss from non-refundable subscriptions during cash-tight months
aspirations: achieve a 'zero-inbox' state by end of day
reduce cognitive load during family time
moral_boundaries: will not trade away other people's privacy for convenience
will not bypass company security protocols even if inconvenient
status_concerns: does not want to appear disorganised in front of clients or team
values being seen as the 'go-to' person for stable operations

life_story.childhood_background: Grew up in a household that valued practical decisions and financial caution in a taiwan knowledge work context.
life_story.education_journey: Completed bachelor level education and learned through a mix of formal training and on-the-job repetition during a mid career operator phase.
life_story.career_path: Moved into field service coordinator work because it rewards reliability and the ability to keep moving parts aligned. Workflow maturity is currently tool heavy, relying on a patchwork of spreadsheets and messaging apps that have grown unwieldy over time.
life_story.family_story: Currently living with parents, which shapes both available time and risk tolerance. Caregiving load is high, requiring strict boundaries between work focus time and family availability. This living arrangement necessitates financial prudence and efficient use of every hour.
life_story.current_daily_routine: Most weekdays are split between coordination work, message follow-up, and protecting small windows of focused time. Mornings involve clearing overnight alerts; afternoons are blocked for deep work; evenings are reserved for family but often interrupted by urgent client issues. Schedule pressure is medium, decision speed is committee slow, and purchase authority is manager approval needed.
recent_life_events: recently reviewed software spend and identified redundant subscriptions
trying to reduce context-switching during the week to improve focus
reassessing workflow due to high cash-flow volatility and upcoming project deadlines
received negative feedback from a manager about missed follow-ups
frustrations: too many tools creating siloed information
manual follow-up consuming prime work hours
context scattered across messages making history hard to retrieve
family responsibilities make admin work feel heavier and less tolerable
new tools often create more setup work than relief, violating the 'quick win' expectation
workflow habits break when too many exceptions pile up, leading to errors
short-term cash pressure makes software decisions feel riskier and requires longer evaluation periods
hidden_needs: wants confidence that a new tool will simplify the week quickly without steep learning curves
needs a reason to trust the workflow before sharing real work into it
seeks justification materials to present to management for approval

behavior_profile.buying_behavior: waits until value is concrete enough to justify habit change; avoids impulse buys; prefers free trials with clear exit paths
information_sources: product demos focusing on practical use cases
comparison notes against current manual processes
social_media_usage: LinkedIn for professional updates and peer validation
WhatsApp groups for immediate team communication and quick advice
behavior_profile.referral_influence: moderate to high; trusts colleagues who have faced similar operational challenges
brand_trust_signals: clear onboarding that doesn't assume technical expertise
specific user stories from similar regional roles
visible privacy stance and compliance certifications
decision_blockers: uncertain ROI within the first month
messy setup requiring data migration from multiple sources
trust gap regarding data handling and third-party integrations
emotional_triggers: relief from admin burden when automation works seamlessly
feeling more in control of follow-up and reducing anxiety about missed tasks
validation from peers confirming the tool's effectiveness

active_pain_points: context scattered across messages hindering retrieval
family responsibilities making admin work feel heavier
new tools often creating more setup work than relief
workflow habits breaking under exception load
short-term cash pressure increasing risk aversion to new subscriptions
latent_pain_points: mental load from tracking too many loose ends
slow drift between promises made to clients and actual follow-up status
anxiety about missing critical details during high-pressure periods
jobs_to_be_done: capture next actions reliably without manual entry
reduce manual follow-up work through automation
avoid dropped tasks via centralized visibility
generate reports easily for manager approval
current_workaround: manual checklists on paper or simple apps

sensitive_reality_layer.social_risk_profile: does not want to look careless with colleagues' or clients' information; fears reputational damage from data mishandling
sensitive_reality_layer.fairness_and_inclusion_profile: expects product language to remain respectful and not assume one type of user; prefers inclusive, neutral terminology
response_boundaries: will not share private third-party data without a clear reason
will not discuss personal family health issues in professional contexts
avoids political discussions in work-related channels
sensitive_reality_layer.public_explanation_preference: plain language with concrete examples; avoids jargon and marketing fluff

audit_evidence_layer.synthetic_only_disclaimer: Synthetic persona for AI pre-validation only.
